pending_agent_instructions.microscopic: Low-cost only; reuse shared prepared structure context and existing artifact bundle 7bc31a3d4c4d_round_01_micro where possible. Run a bounded conformer ensemble check to probe conformer-selection: (1) discovery: list_available_conformers from the shared prepared structure; select up to 5 lowest-energy distinct conformers (including conformer_id=5 if available). (2) execution: run_conformer_bundle on those conformers with S0 optimization + vertical excitations (states 1–5) at the same low-cost settings as baseline_bundle. (3) report: for each conformer provide S1 energy (eV), f, and whether lowest_state_is_bright; summarize the spread (max–min) in S1 energy and in f across conformers and flag any conformers with notably reduced f (e.g., f<0.1) or different bright-state ordering.

hypothesis_pool.name: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The molecule is a large, highly arylated conjugated system (10 aromatic rings) with ~10 rotatable bonds and multiple exocyclic C=C linkages; in dilute solution these torsions can enable internal conversion via torsional/vibronic channels, while aggregation can rigidify the structure and reduce nonradiative rates. The prepared-structure proxies indicate high planarity but still substantial torsion candidates/rotors, consistent with RIM-type AIE.

hypothesis_pool.name: Excited-state symmetry breaking / conformational planarization–rigidification in aggregate (conformer selection) leading to higher radiative yield
hypothesis_pool.rationale: A very extended π-system with multiple aryl substituents can host multiple near-degenerate conformers and excited-state relaxation pathways; aggregation may select/lock a more emissive conformer and reduce access to dark geometries. Conformer dispersion proxy suggests multiple conformers were found, so conformer-selection effects are plausible alongside generic RIM.

hypothesis_pool.name: Aggregate-state emissive species (excimer/exciplex-like) dominating emission
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Large polyaryl surfaces and high aromatic count can promote π–π contacts that sometimes yield new aggregate emissive states. However, the molecule is very bulky (many phenyls) which can also hinder strong face-to-face stacking; without packing data this remains a weaker initial guess.

state_snapshot.current_hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation
planner_diagnosis_history: Task: infer the most likely AIE mechanism for the provided C58H40N2S molecule from structure-level cues and quick electronic-structure evidence. Leading working hypothesis is RIM (torsional/rotor restriction) because the structure contains many aryl rotors and multiple torsion candidates that can enable nonradiative decay in solution but become hindered in aggregates. This remains uncertain because (i) the prepared structure appears quite planar (planarity_proxy ~0.97), so the key motion may be localized torsions rather than global twisting, (ii) aggregate packing could instead create new emissive aggregate states (excimer/exciplex) or strong conformer selection effects, and (iii) without S1 character/oscillator strength and low-cost indicators of dark-state access, the balance between RIM vs aggregate-state emission is unclear. Shared prepared structure context is available (selected conformer, XYZ/SDF paths), enabling downstream macro/microscopic evidence collection. Specialized agents can: Macro—do motif/property-based qualitative assessment and propose discriminating signatures; Microscopic—run low-cost aTB S0 optimization and bounded S1 vertical excitations (few states) on the provided structure. Microscopic first round must stay bounded because the system is large (101 atoms) and exhaustive DFT/TDDFT conformer/geometry searches would be expensive and not aligned with the baseline policy. Both macro and microscopic evidence are needed to connect structural flexibility/packing propensity (macro) with S1 character/transition strength and sensitivity to torsion (micro).

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation. Latest evidence modestly strengthens (but does not resolve) this hypothesis: macro topology shows a highly arylated scaffold with ~10 rotatable torsions consistent with phenyl-rotor/aryl–vinyl twisting channels that can be immobilized in aggregates; microscopic baseline electronic structure shows a strongly allowed S1 (E(S1)≈3.06 eV, f≈1.03; lowest state is bright), which argues against a ‘dark S1 rescued only by rigidification’ subtype but remains compatible with RIM operating via suppression of internal conversion through torsional motion. Key remaining uncertainty: whether low-energy torsional motions actually modulate S1 properties or open efficient nonradiative pathways (and whether aggregate emission could instead be excimer/exciplex dominated). Best next action: run a bounded torsion-snapshot sensitivity probe on up to two key dihedrals (±20°, ≤4 snapshots) to directly test whether modest torsional changes significantly affect S1 energy/oscillator strength, providing the highest information gain within low-cost constraints.

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation. Latest evidence weakens the specific “torsional-rotor gating” support for RIM: the targeted ±20° torsion snapshots on one central aryl–vinyl dihedral show essentially no change in S1 vertical energy or oscillator strength (ΔE~0.0002 eV, Δf~0.0023; still lowest_state_is_bright). Key remaining uncertainty: the tested dihedral/window may not correspond to the actual motion(s) that enable nonradiative decay (could be other dihedrals, larger amplitude twists, multi-coordinate motion, or excited-state relaxation/MECI access not captured by vertical snapshots), and aggregate-state excimer/exciplex vs rigidification is still un-discriminated without external photophysics. Best next action now is Verifier: we have exhausted the highest-value low-cost internal discriminator available (bounded torsion snapshots), so external experimental/literature signatures are the most direct way to adjudicate RIM vs excimer/exciplex for this specific molecule.

External supervision (verifier) provided no usable evidence cards due to a hard retrieval failure (403 region restriction), so it neither supports nor conflicts with the current leading hypothesis. Internally, macro structure remains consistent with an RIM-capable scaffold (many rotors, bulky polyaryl), but the key new microscopic discriminator weakens the specific sub-claim that a central aryl–vinyl torsion strongly modulates the emissive S1: ±20° torsion snapshots gave negligible changes (ΔE~0.0002 eV, Δf~0.0023; torsion_sensitive=false) and S1 is already very bright (f~1.03). This is not strong evidence against RIM overall (nonradiative decay can be governed by other coordinates and excited-state relaxation pathways not accessible here), but it reduces confidence in “torsional-rotor gating” as the primary lever. With verifier unavailable, remaining uncertainty is dominated by capability limits (no excited-state relaxation/PES, no packing/aggregate modeling, and no external photophysics). A conservative next step is a bounded microscopic follow-up (still low-cost) to test a different central dihedral and/or larger torsional amplitude to see whether any coordinate shows meaningful vertical-state sensitivity; otherwise we risk stagnation.

Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation. Latest evidence weakens direct support for a torsion-gated RIM mechanism: two distinct central aryl–vinyl dihedrals probed (dih_0_1_2_3 at ±20°; dih_12_13_14_15 at ±20/40°) show only very small changes in vertical S1 energy/oscillator strength (ΔE up to ~0.014 eV; Δf up to ~0.008) and the lowest state remains bright. Key remaining uncertainty: RIM could still operate via other coordinates (multi-dihedral twisting, phenyl flapping, out-of-plane modes) and/or via excited-state relaxation pathways (e.g., access to CI/MECI) that are not captured by vertical-only snapshots; alternatively aggregate emission could involve excimer/exciplex species, which cannot be discriminated without external photophysics. Best next action: perform a bounded conformer-ensemble follow-up (still low-cost) to test whether multiple low-energy ground-state conformers have substantially different vertical emissive properties, supporting a conformer-selection/locking variant of RIM versus the current torsion-insensitivity result.

macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From topology: the molecule is a large, highly arylated, multi-phenyl (10 aromatic rings) conjugated system with one fused heteroaromatic (nsnc) unit (3 hetero atoms). With ~10 rotatable bonds, the dominant intramolecular motions are torsions about aryl–aryl single bonds and torsions around exocyclic vinyl/alkenyl linkages (stilbene-like C=C connecting aryls) that enable phenyl-rotor motion and twisting. Geometry proxies indicate high planarity_proxy (~0.97) and compactness_proxy (~0.95), suggesting a relatively flattened, shape-persistent conformer in the prepared structure, but still with multiple torsional degrees of freedom that can be hindered in aggregates (supporting RIM plausibility). For packing: the very bulky multi-phenyl periphery and high branch_point_count imply steric shielding that can reduce cofacial π–π stacking, though large planar π-surface and high aromatic count can still enable some slipped stacking; thus excimer is possible but not guaranteed and would likely require specific cofacial contacts. Discriminating signatures should therefore target (i) viscosity/temperature dependence (RIM), (ii) concentration- and excitation-dependent red-shift/broadening (excimer/exciplex), and (iii) evidence of multiple emissive conformers/dual emission and time-resolved spectral evolution (conformer-selection/excimer).
Capability limit note: Will rely only on deterministic structural/topological interpretation of the provided canonical SMILES and the given proxies (rotatable bonds, aromatic rings, hetero atoms, planarity/compactness/dispersion). No aggregate-state modeling, no packing simulations, and no new heavy computations will be invoked.

last_planner_decision.diagnosis: Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM: phenyl-rotor/torsional restriction) suppressing nonradiative decay upon aggregation. Latest evidence weakens direct support for a torsion-gated RIM mechanism: two distinct central aryl–vinyl dihedrals probed (dih_0_1_2_3 at ±20°; dih_12_13_14_15 at ±20/40°) show only very small changes in vertical S1 energy/oscillator strength (ΔE up to ~0.014 eV; Δf up to ~0.008) and the lowest state remains bright. Key remaining uncertainty: RIM could still operate via other coordinates (multi-dihedral twisting, phenyl flapping, out-of-plane modes) and/or via excited-state relaxation pathways (e.g., access to CI/MECI) that are not captured by vertical-only snapshots; alternatively aggregate emission could involve excimer/exciplex species, which cannot be discriminated without external photophysics. Best next action: perform a bounded conformer-ensemble follow-up (still low-cost) to test whether multiple low-energy ground-state conformers have substantially different vertical emissive properties, supporting a conformer-selection/locking variant of RIM versus the current torsion-insensitivity result.
